epigraph.text: Direct my footsteps according to your word; let no sin rule over me.

content.text:
A line extends infinitely in both directions, but many geometric figures 
involve only portions of lines. In this section, we define segments and 
rays—bounded and half-bounded portions of lines that form the building 
blocks of triangles, polygons, and more complex figures.

content.title: Line Segment
content.content:
A **line segment** (or simply **segment**) is 
the part of a line consisting of two points, called **endpoints**, 
and all points between them.

The segment with endpoints $A$ and $B$ is denoted $\overline{AB}$ or 
$\overline{BA}$.

content.text:
Unlike a line, a segment has a definite **length**. 
The length of $\overline{AB}$ is written $AB$ (without the bar). Notice:

items: $\overline{AB}$ is a geometric object (a set of points)
$AB$ is a number (the distance from $A$ to $B$)

content.title: Congruent Segments
content.content:
Two segments are **congruent** if they have 
the same length. We write $\overline{AB} \cong \overline{CD}$ to indicate 
that segments $\overline{AB}$ and $\overline{CD}$ are congruent.

content.text:
The symbol "$\cong$" means "is congruent to." Note that congruence 
applies to geometric figures, while equality applies to numbers:
These statements convey the same information in different ways.

How do we measure segments? The **Ruler Postulate** 
establishes that we can assign coordinates to points on a line.

content.title: Ruler Postulate
content.content:
The points on a line can be put into one-to-one correspondence with the 
real numbers. The distance between two points is the absolute value of 
the difference of their coordinates.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
If point $A$ has coordinate $a$ and point $B$ has coordinate $b$, then:
\[
 AB = |a - b| = |b - a|
\]

content.title: Finding segment lengths
content.problem:
On a number line, $A$ has coordinate $-3$ and $B$ has coordinate $5$. 
Find $AB$.

content.title: Segment Addition Postulate
content.content:
If $B$ is between $A$ and $C$, then $AB + BC = AC$.

Conversely, if $AB + BC = AC$, then $B$ is between $A$ and $C$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Using segment addition
content.problem:
Point $M$ is between $P$ and $Q$. If $PM = 3x + 2$, $MQ = 2x + 3$, and 
$PQ = 25$, find $x$, $PM$, and $MQ$.
content.solution:
By the Segment Addition Postulate:
\[
 PM + MQ = PQ
\]
\[
 (3x + 2) + (2x + 3) = 25
\]
\[
 5x + 5 = 25
\]
\[
 x = 4
\]

Therefore, $PM = 3(4) + 2 = 14$ and $MQ = 2(4) + 3 = 11$.

Check: $14 + 11 = 25$

content.title: Midpoint
content.content:
The **midpoint** of a segment is the point that 
divides the segment into two congruent segments.

If $M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$, then $AM = MB = \frac{1}{2}AB$.

content.title: Segment Bisector
content.content:
A **segment bisector** is any line, segment, 
ray, or plane that passes through the midpoint of a segment.

content.title: Finding a midpoint
content.problem:
$M$ is the midpoint of $\overline{AB}$. If $AM = 3x - 1$ and $MB = 2x + 3$, 
find $AB$.
content.solution:
Since $M$ is the midpoint, $AM = MB$:
\[
 3x - 1 = 2x + 3
\]
\[
 x = 4
\]

So $AM = 3(4) - 1 = 11$ and $MB = 2(4) + 3 = 11$.

Therefore, $AB = AM + MB = 11 + 11 = 22$.

content.title: Ray
content.content:
A **ray** is a portion of a line consisting of one 
endpoint and all points on one side of that endpoint.

The ray with endpoint $A$ passing through $B$ is denoted $\overrightarrow{AB}$.

content.text:
**Important:** The order of letters matters for rays! $\overrightarrow{AB}$ 
and $\overrightarrow{BA}$ are different rays (unless $A = B$).

content.title: Opposite Rays
content.content:
**Opposite rays** are two rays that share the 
same endpoint and together form a line.

content.text:
Rays $\overrightarrow{BA}$ and $\overrightarrow{BC}$ are opposite rays 
because they share endpoint $B$ and together form line $\overleftrightarrow{AC}$.

marginNotes.text:
They shall make an ark of acacia wood. 
Two cubits and a half shall be its length...
